preFix.title: Repairing a delaminating concrete floor screed
preFix.subtitle: Cut out and recast a section of screed that has separated from the slab
preFix.excerpt: How to identify and repair a section of floor screed that has delaminated from the concrete slab beneath it, covering how to map the hollow area, saw-cut the perimeter, break out the failed screed, treat the slab, and lay a new patch of sand-and-cement screed to match the surrounding level.
preFix.sourceNotes: BS 8204-1: Screeds, bases and in-situ floorings. BRE Good Repair Guide 11 (floor screed defects). City and Guilds Level 2 Plastering. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: A delaminating screed flexes under foot traffic, cracks along the hollow boundary, and eventually collapses. It is identified by a dull resonant sound when tapped with a knuckle or a coin. Like tapping on a drum rather than a flat solid surface. The repair requires cutting back to sound screed on all sides. Not just filling the crack. Any unsupported area of the delaminated screed will fail again at the same boundary.

Sharp sand and Portland cement for the new screed mix. SBR bonding agent. PVA adhesive. Rapid-setting screed repair mortar is an alternative for small areas. A straight metal screed rail or a timber batten to level the patch surface.

Tap systematically across the floor in a grid pattern and mark all hollow-sounding areas with chalk or pencil. Extend the investigation beyond the visible crack or surface damage; hollow areas are consistently larger than they appear. Mark a cut line at least 50 mm into sound screed on all sides of the hollow zone.
Step 2, saw-cut the perimeter and break out the old screed

Fit a diamond blade to the angle grinder and saw-cut along the marked perimeter to the full depth of the screed. The saw-cut creates a clean vertical edge for the new screed to butt against; without it. The repair edge will be ragged and liable to further cracking. Break out the delaminated screed with the SDS drill chisel and remove all debris. Vacuum the slab surface clean.
Step 3, treat the slab and apply bonding agent

Check the exposed slab for any contamination. Clean with a stiff brush. Brush the SBR bonding agent diluted 1:1 with water onto the slab surface and up the saw-cut walls. Allow to become tacky. Mix a slurry of SBR and cement powder and brush it over the slab just before the screed goes down. This cement-SBR slurry provides extra grip between the slab and the new screed.
Step 4, lay and level the new screed

Mix the screed at 3:1 sharp sand to cement. It should be stiff enough to hold a footprint when stepped on. Fill the prepared area in one pass. Use a screed rail or a long timber batten spanning the sound screed on each side to rule the surface level. Drag the rule across the top of the sound screed to bring the new patch flush. Tamp the surface to consolidate, then rule again. Apply a steel trowel finish if the area will be tiled. A float finish if it will be carpeted.

Protect the fresh screed from foot traffic for 24 hours and from floor covering installation for at least 7 days. Cover with polythene sheeting in warm weather to prevent rapid drying, which weakens the surface. If the underlying cause of the original delamination is not resolved, the repair will fail again.

attrs.fix: Apply a self-levelling floor compound over the patch once the screed has cured (at least 3 days). Self-levelling compound finds its own level and can fill low spots up to 10 mm.
attrs.cause: The screed mix was too wet and slumped slightly as it cured.
attrs.symptom: New patch is lower than the surrounding floor by 2 to 4 mm

attrs.fix: Rake out the crack, prime with diluted SBR, and fill with a flexible floor repair compound. This type of movement joint crack at a patch edge is common and largely cosmetic where the screed is otherwise well bonded.
attrs.cause: The saw-cut edge was not clean or the new screed was not pressed firmly against the existing screed at the perimeter.
attrs.symptom: Crack appears at the junction between new and old screed within a few weeks
